issue_body:
REQUEST MOTIVATION

I suggest switching to the 8.0 language version and enabling "Nullable reference types". You need to start with basic things like the **openra.game** project.

The change will reduce the number of NRE errors, will remove the "! = Null" checks and possibly improve performance.

PROPOSED SOLUTION

Allow lang ver 8.0 in OpenRA.Game/OpenRA.Game.csproj
```XML
    <LangVersion>8.0</LangVersion>
    <!-- In future change this to "enable"-->
    <Nullable>warnings</Nullable>
    <!-- Then project will be annotated, change this to "true"-->
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>false</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
```
EXPECTED SIDE EFFECTS

Nothings

ALTERNATIVES

Use JetBrains.Annotations nuget package
pr_title: Enable nullable reference types on ~100 types.
pr_body:
Closes #19514

Begin a progressive move towards enabling nullable reference types by targeting types across the solution with the fewest dependencies on other solution code and enabling nullable reference types on that type, or perhaps the whole file. There are ~2600 types across the solution.

- Introduce Unforgiving, a set of methods that throw rather than returning null. Useful when calling code is never expecting the null case to be possible.
- If a type was unused, prefer to just delete it.
- Remove ArgumentNullException as this is rarely used anyway.
- In HttpQueryBuilder, implement IEnumerable<KeyValuePair<string, string>> properly at the same time as switching the Parameter type to KeyValuePair.
- Switch Array.Empty<Type>() to Type.EmptyTypes which is more widely used.

----

As a project we currently have `<Nullable>disable</Nullable>` employed across the solution. Now that we've moved to a C# version that supports nullable reference types we could opt to enable these.

Enabling them for the entire solution in one-go would be too large a task, but this PR shows how we could manage it progressivly by using `#nullable enable` directives to slowly introduce it throughout the code. Locally I have generated a dependency-tree for types within the solution and targeted those as the first ones to introduce nullability on. By starting from the bottom we maximise the amount of depencies that are already nullable-aware. If we started at the top the dependencies may not be nullable-aware. That could result in rework in enabling nullablity on a class lower in the dependncy tree may force you to revisit its dependencies and update them again. As we introduce more nullablity it's possible we may need to go back to already converted types and change the nullability annotations if they weren't "correct" on the first pass as a result of learning more about how the dependency is used, but again starting from the bottom should help reduce unessessary rework here.

Classes not yet nullable aware won't get warnings, so existing code and PRs can continue unaffected and we don't need to halt ongoing work for a migration effort.

I spent several hours on this PR (didn't track exactly) but the ~100 types cover and ~2500 types to go gives a ballpark total effort on the order of magnitude of 150 hours to get through the whole solution.

I've put togther this PR to give a taste of how this might look like if we did want to make this migration, so people can hopefully make an informed choice about whether this is something we want to employ project wide or not. Although we'd likely have to migrate many more types before getting to some of the more interesting ones, I will leave comments to call out the early bugs that these changes have already made visible.

If we did complete a migration, then the last PR would remove all `#nullable` directives from files and set `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>` across the solution instead - so the file level directives are only required as we migrate and are not a permenant fixture.

A few other takeaways:
- Most classes can remain not-null. In many files adding the directive was all that was needed. Very few members are actually annotated as nullable.
- Even if adding the directive reveals no warnings, a scan through the code to check if it treats anything it uses a potientally null is still required so you can annotate them. You're not done merely by resolving the warnings.
- Interfaces are a great example where you need to check how they are used in practise and apply annotations based on that - but once we get to their concrete classes we might need to come back if the first pass didn't get the right shape.
